headline: BaseballPro77 Just Broke the League
subheadline: Nineteen home runs, 39 RBI, and a 7-2 demolition of Wisconsin Rapids — someone call the record books, because Week 4 just happened.
body:
## Thriller of the Week

### Canberra Wombats vs. Riverside Roughnecks — 4-4 Tie

You know that scene in *No Country for Old Men* where Anton Chigurh flips the coin and neither side really wins? That's the **Canberra Wombats** and **Riverside Roughnecks** splitting a 4-4 tie this week. The **Roughnecks** had the weapons — Jacob Misiorowski punched out 14 batters, Raisel Iglesias racked up 3 NSVH, and they posted the highest OBP in the entire league at .368 — but their offense couldn't capitalize when it mattered, leaving runs on the table like a bad tipper at a diner. Meanwhile, the **Wombats** desperately needed a win to crawl out of the basement (more on that later), got solid contributions from Jo Adell and Pete Crow-Armstrong, and still couldn't push through a pitching staff that posted a 4.35 ERA and only 3 QS.

The stakes here could not have been more uncomfortable for both sides. The **Wombats** needed this win the way Tom Hanks needed that volleyball in *Cast Away* — it was supposed to be their lifeline — and instead they're still treading water at the bottom of the standings. The **Roughnecks**, sitting at 10th in the power rankings, let a genuinely winnable matchup bleed into a split, which is the fantasy equivalent of leaving points on the field in a close playoff game. Both teams now enter Week 5 with must-perform rosters and very little margin for error.

---

## The Week's Defining Moment

Let me be direct with you: what **BaseballPro77** did to the **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** this week was not a fantasy baseball victory. It was an archaeological event. Nineteen home runs. Thirty-nine RBI. A 2.33 ERA. A 7-2 final score. I've been doing this long enough to know when something is genuinely historic, and this was the *Secretariat at Belmont* of fantasy baseball weeks — so dominant it stopped being competitive and started being something you just watched with your mouth open.

Mike Trout went absolutely nuclear: 9 runs, 5 home runs, 9 RBI. Munetaka Murakami added 4 home runs and 9 RBI. Austin Riley chipped in 3 home runs and 9 RBI. That's 12 home runs and 27 RBI from three guys alone (the other 7 HR came from the rest of the roster), and the supporting cast didn't even need to show up for the record books to be rewritten. The pitching side was equally absurd — Gavin Williams and George Kirby anchored a staff that posted a 2.33 ERA, good for the best ERA in the league this week. Oh, and **BaseballPro77** also added Matt Strahm off waivers from the **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs**, because apparently they needed more weapons.

The **Reapers** — to their modest credit — weren't completely invisible. Mason Miller was outstanding in relief, posting a filthy 0.50 WHIP with 4 NSVH, and Bryan Woo delivered 2 quality starts. But you know the old saying about bringing a knife to a gunfight? The **Reapers** brought a butter knife to a bazooka fight, surrendering 39 runs scored against a pitching staff that simply never gave **BaseballPro77** a reason to slow down. The final category tally was 7-2. It wasn't close. It wasn't supposed to be.

---

## Records Broken

**BaseballPro77** didn't just win this week — they rewrote the league record books in real time. The 19 home runs set a new single-week team record for home runs, and the 39 RBI set a new single-week team record for runs batted in. Both marks were set in Week 4, both are now held exclusively by **BaseballPro77**, and both were achieved in the same matchup against the same overwhelmed opponent. The previous HR and RBI records are now jointly held by **BaseballPro77** themselves, which is the most baller way to hold a record. This is the fantasy equivalent of someone breaking the single-season scoring record while also breaking the assists record in the same game — it doesn't happen often, and when it does, you remember it.

---

## Matchup Deep-Dives

### Albuquerque Bombers def. Sugar Land Skeeters, 5-4

This was the closest thing to an upset of the week. The **Sugar Land Skeeters** came in with the best four-week resume in the league — category wins of 9, 8, 8, and 4 — and got edged by an **Albuquerque Bombers** squad that simply out-ran them. The **Bombers** led the entire league in runs scored with 40 (yes, the entire league), rode Byron Buxton's 3-HR week, and stole 7 bases to compensate for a rotation that posted a 4.03 ERA. The **Skeeters** countered with the best strikeout total in the league at 72 K (David Weathers alone punched out 18 batters), but a 6.05 ERA will lose you categories in a hurry regardless of how many hitters you miss.

What does this mean going forward? The **Skeeters** drop to #2 in the power rankings but still have the best sustained record in the league — this is a one-week blip, not a pattern. The **Bombers**, meanwhile, made four waiver moves this week (more on that below) and are starting to look like a team that's figured out its identity. If their new rotation pieces deliver, they could quietly become the most dangerous 6th-place team in the league.

### Abbotsford-Colby Twins def. Hiroshima Radiation, 6-4

This matchup was a genuine tragedy for the **Hiroshima Radiation**, and I say that with complete sincerity. Tarik Skubal and Nathan Eovaldi both posted sub-1.50 ERAs. The team ERA was 2.81 — legitimately elite. And yet they lost the matchup because a -1 NSVH tanked the pitching side and handed the **Twins** a category they shouldn't have needed. It's the fantasy equivalent of driving a perfect race and blowing a tire on the last lap.

The **Twins** won because José Ramírez was an absolute menace — 4 HR, 5 SB, and a .533 OBP that made him look like a cheat code — and Yordan Alvarez added 4 HR of his own. With the **Twins** leading the league in both home runs (14, second only to **BaseballPro77**) and stolen bases (tied for first with 8), they have one of the most dangerous offensive profiles in the game right now. Reid Detmers' sparkling ERA is also a quiet revelation. The **Radiation** needs to solve the saves situation before it costs them another winnable week.

### Athletic Park Dragons def. North Correa, 5-2

Aaron Judge hit 5 home runs, James Wood contributed throughout the lineup, and the **Athletic Park Dragons** finally got their rotation to behave — a 3.01 ERA and 5 QS will win you categories more often than not. This felt like a team clicking into gear, which is either encouraging or terrifying depending on where you sit. The **Dragons** also tied for the league lead in stolen bases with 8, so the offensive profile is genuinely balanced.

The **North Correa** situation is tricky. They went 5-2 in categories against 7-category **Dragons**, and their own numbers weren't bad — 36 runs, 33 RBI, 8 stolen bases. But a 5.56 ERA is a rotation-level problem, not a bad week, and Chris Sale's strong WHIP can only carry so much. The Austin Wells upgrade at catcher is smart and necessary (Realmuto has been a liability), but the pitching staff needs help before Nico Hoerner's RBI production starts feeling wasted.

### BaseballPro77 def. Wisconsin Rapids Reapers, 7-2

Covered extensively above. Move along. Nothing to see here except the greatest single-week offensive performance in league history.

### General Hospital def. Benchwarmers, 8-2

This was less a matchup and more a mercy rule situation. **General Hospital** posted 6 quality starts (the second-most in the league this week), a 2.68 ERA, a league-leading 0.96 WHIP, and 36 RBI (second in the league). Will Warren was genuinely excellent — 17 strikeouts and a 1.69 ERA in a week where the rotation needed a signature performance. The **Benchwarmers** had Michael King pitching out of his mind with an 0.82 ERA, and Cody Bellinger posted an .481 OBP, but you cannot win a 10-category matchup with 4 home runs and 45 hits. The math doesn't work.

The **Benchwarmers** are in genuine trouble, and this week's roster moves (covered below) suggest the front office may be in disarray. **General Hospital**, meanwhile, just added Kumar Rocker to a staff that already has the best WHIP in the league. I'll say it plainly: if Rocker delivers anything near his ceiling, this rotation becomes untouchable.

### Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs def. Buttville Skidmarks, 6-3

Paul Skenes posted a 0.90 ERA and a 0.50 WHIP this week, which are numbers so absurd they sound made up. Combine that with Imanaga and Framber Valdez contributing to 8 QS — the highest total in the entire league — and a 2.49 ERA (second-lowest in the league, just behind **BaseballPro77**), and you understand why the **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** are a genuine contender. The **Skidmarks** actually led the league in innings pitched at 77.2 and fought hard, with Junior Caminero posting 3 home runs, but MacKenzie Gore's 6.52 ERA and Kevin Gausman's 4.09 ERA torpedoed the pitching categories before they ever got started.

The **Wonderdogs** are now up to #4 in the power rankings, and the roster management on the pitching side has been legitimately impressive. The concern is the waiver activity this week — losing Matt Strahm and Jack Flaherty while adding several depth pieces is a lot of turnover for a team that was already working with bad luck. The offense is starting to contribute, which matters. But they need stability, not churn.

---

## Lucky/Unlucky Team of the Week

**Lucky Team: Benchwarmers.** Look, I like the **Benchwarmers** as people (presumably), but the luck delta situation here is genuinely unsustainable. Despite posting only 4 home runs, a 4.40 ERA, and among the lowest strikeout totals of any team this week, they somehow managed to win 2 categories. Their +1.21 luck delta is the most inflated number in the league — this is a team that is consistently winning categories it statistically has no business winning, and at some point the regression is coming like a freight train. Enjoy the 2 category wins while they last, because the math is coming for you.

**Unlucky Team: Abbotsford-Colby Twins.** Here's what makes this maddening — the **Twins** won their matchup 6-4 this week, which sounds fine. But their luck delta of -0.61 is the worst in the league, meaning their category output consistently *outperforms* their actual results. They are producing like a top-five team and getting rewarded like an eighth-place one. José Ramírez and Yordan Alvarez are two of the most productive fantasy players in the league right now. The schedule will eventually balance out. When it does, this team is going to go on a run.

---

## Manager Spotlight: North Correa

**North Correa** is the kind of team that makes you want to sit down and have a serious conversation over coffee. They've got real pieces — Chris Sale is elite when healthy, Nico Hoerner is producing RBI at a rate that surprises people, and Austin Wells at catcher is a meaningful upgrade over the Realmuto era. They're sitting at 9th in the power rankings, which feels about right, but the gap between 9th and 6th is not as wide as it looks if they can solve the starting pitching ERA problem.

Here's the thing: **North Correa** went toe-to-toe with the **Athletic Park Dragons** this week — matching them in runs (both had 36), narrowly losing in RBI (33 vs. 32), and tying in stolen bases (8 each). The offense is not the problem. A 5.56 ERA is the problem. It's been the problem. The Wells-for-Realmuto move shows a front office willing to identify weaknesses and address them directly, which is a good sign. But if the rotation doesn't stabilize before the playoff push begins, all those Ramírez-level contributions from the supporting cast are going to feel like they're being wasted. This is a team with a real ceiling if the pitching gets right — and right now, that's a genuinely interesting "if."

---

## Trade Value Watch

**Mike Trout, BaseballPro77 — HOLD.** Nine runs, 5 home runs, 9 RBI in a single week — that's not a hot streak, that's a reminder of what Trout looks like when he's locked in. **BaseballPro77** has no incentive to move him, and any offer that makes this trade worth doing for the receiving team would have to be genuinely outrageous. Lock him in for the season and enjoy the ride.

**Kumar Rocker, General Hospital — BUY.** He hasn't thrown a pitch yet in this league, but **General Hospital** already has the best WHIP in the league and a 2.68 ERA this week, and Rocker slots into that rotation as a potential difference-maker if he delivers anything close to his upside. If you can acquire him before the returns come in, you're buying at the right time — the moment he logs a dominant start, the asking price doubles.

**Mason Miller, Wisconsin Rapids Reapers — SELL.** A 0.50 WHIP and 4 NSVH in a week where his team got historically embarrassed tells you everything you need to know about Miller's individual value relative to his team context. The **Reapers** are going to keep losing matchups, which means Miller's NSVH opportunities could dry up or become situationally irrelevant. Any contender desperate for saves should be calling the **Reapers** right now, and the **Reapers** should be listening.

---

## Power Rankings

1. **BaseballPro77** | Record-setting 19 HR and 39 RBI week with a 2.33 ERA confirms this is the most complete roster in the league right now — Trout, Murakami, and Riley are a top-three power trio in the game [UP]
2. **Sugar Land Skeeters** | Still the most consistent team over four weeks with category wins totals of 9, 8, 8, and 4 — one off week doesn't erase the best overall resume in the league, and Weathers (18 K) reminded everyone the staff has weapons [SAME]
3. **General Hospital** | An 8-2 demolition powered by Will Warren (17 K, 1.69 ERA), league-leading 6 QS, and a 2.68 ERA — this rotation is the best in the league and Kumar Rocker hasn't even thrown a pitch yet [UP]
4. **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** | Paul Skenes (0.90 ERA, 0.50 WHIP), Imanaga, and Framber Valdez combined for a filthy 8 QS and a 2.49 ERA — the pitching staff is genuinely elite and the offense is starting to show up around it [UP]
5. **Athletic Park Dragons** | Aaron Judge (5 HR), James Wood, and José Caballero anchored another strong offensive week, and the rotation finally delivered a 3.01 ERA with 5 QS — this feels like the team clicking into gear [SAME]
6. **Albuquerque Bombers** | Won 5-4 with Byron Buxton (3 HR) and a balanced lineup carrying the load — four waiver adds show an aggressive front office, and the 8 category wins in Week 5 history suggests a hot streak may be brewing [SAME]
7. **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** | José Ramírez (4 HR, 5 SB, .533 OBP) and Yordan Alvarez (4 HR) is one of the most dangerous one-two punches in the league, and Reid Detmers' 1.29 ERA hints at a rotation stabilizing [UP]
8. **Hiroshima Radiation** | Tarik Skubal and Nathan Eovaldi both posted sub-1.50 ERAs and the team ERA was a sparkling 2.81, but a -1 NSVH tanked the pitching side and cost them the matchup — the saves situation is a real problem [DOWN]
9. **North Correa** | Lost 5-2 despite Chris Sale (0.86 WHIP) and solid contributions from Nico Hoerner (11 RBI) — the pitching ERA of 5.56 is the culprit, and Austin Wells replacing Realmuto is a meaningful upgrade worth watching [SAME]
10. **Riverside Roughnecks** | A 4-4 tie when a win was there for the taking is the story — Jacob Misiorowski (14 K) and Raisel Iglesias (3 NSVH) showed real weapons, but the offense left runs on the table all week [SAME]
11. **Canberra Wombats** | Jo Adell (2 HR, 8 RBI) and Pete Crow-Armstrong were bright spots, but a 4.35 ERA, only 3 QS, and a .283 OBP won't win matchups — adding JR Ritchie while dropping Sasaki is a puzzling rotation decision [SAME]
12. **Buttville Skidmarks** | Junior Caminero (3 HR) had a strong week but MacKenzie Gore's 6.52 ERA and Gausman's 4.09 ERA tanked the pitching categories — six category wins added to Week 5's history suggests a bounce-back may be coming [UP]
13. **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** | Got historically embarrassed by BaseballPro77's record-setting week, but Bryan Woo (2 QS) and Mason Miller (4 NSVH, 0.50 WHIP) showed this team has real relief-side assets to build around [SAME]
14. **Benchwarmers** | Michael King (0.82 ERA) was outstanding and Cody Bellinger (.481 OBP) continues to be productive, but 4 HR and only 45 hits in a 10-category format means an 8-2 drubbing was almost inevitable — the roster needs a power injection badly [DOWN]

---

## Waiver Wire

**[A] General Hospital** — Added Kumar Rocker; dropped Jack Leiter, Janson Junk — This is the move of the week, and it's not particularly close. Rocker slots into an already elite rotation that posted 6 QS and a 2.68 ERA this week, and if he delivers even half of his upside, **General Hospital's** staff becomes genuinely untouchable. Leiter was expendable and Junk is — well, the name says it. This is how you build a dynasty.

**[B] Albuquerque Bombers** — Added Keaton Winn, Braxton Garrett, Noelvi Marte, Josh Jung; dropped Sam Antonacci, Jonathan Loáisiga — Four adds in one week is the most aggressive roster management we've seen this season, and it's the kind of move that either looks brilliant in October or gets you roasted in the group chat by July. Winn and Garrett address a rotation that desperately needed depth, while Marte and Jung patch the corner infield holes that have been costing the **Bombers** counting stats. The Loáisiga drop stings — he was a useful late-inning arm — but the front office clearly decided the saves were covered and went all-in on volume.

**[B] North Correa** — Added Austin Wells; dropped J.T. Realmuto — Clean, smart, no-drama roster management. Wells has better counting stats upside and Realmuto has been a liability at catcher all season. Sometimes the best move is the obvious one, and **North Correa** made it without overthinking it.

**[B] Riverside Roughnecks** — Added Sam Bachman, Victor Vodnik; dropped Mark Vientos — Doubling down on bullpen depth makes complete sense given how the **Roughnecks** are constructed, and Bachman in particular has legitimate closing upside. Losing Vientos hurts positional flexibility in the corner spots, but when your identity is built around saves and holds, you protect that above everything else.

**[C] Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** — Added Chase Dollander, Justin Bruihl, Erik Miller, Ildemaro Vargas; dropped Jack Flaherty, Justin Sterner, Taylor Clarke, Matt Strahm — This is a lot of roster churn for a team that already had good pitching and bad luck, and losing Strahm — who walked directly onto **BaseballPro77's** roster, because of course he did — is going to sting. Flaherty had obvious upside as a streamer with a big ceiling. Dollander is a fascinating flier, but this is the kind of move that makes sense in your head at 11 PM on a Tuesday and looks different in the morning. The **Wonderdogs** need stability right now, not a rotation renovation.

**[D] Canberra Wombats** — Added JR Ritchie; dropped Roki Sasaki — I'm going to need someone to explain this one to me slowly, using small words, because I cannot process it. Roki Sasaki is a legitimate ace-caliber arm. JR Ritchie is not. If Sasaki gets picked up by a contender — and someone will pick him up, I promise you — this could be the worst single transaction of the entire season. The **Wombats** are already at the bottom of the standings and desperately need pitching. This is the wrong direction at the worst possible time.

**[D] Benchwarmers** — Added Casey Schmitt; dropped Josh Naylor, Max Scherzer, Javier Assad — Okay. So. The **Benchwarmers** dropped Max Scherzer and Javier Assad in the same week. Together. Simultaneously. After already losing the matchup 8-2 largely because of pitching. And replaced them with Casey Schmitt, who is a fine defensive third baseman in real baseball but is not going to save a fantasy roster. This isn't roster management — this is a fire sale at a store that was already struggling. If there's a plan here, I'd genuinely love to hear it, because from the outside this looks like a team that has decided to wave the white flag before we've even hit the quarter pole of the season.
